pr_review_comment_body: I'm not in love with typed_method's interface, but as it's only a fallback for people that can't use Python 3, I don't really care. On the other hand, typed_signal should be pretty.
And also change the values from what they were in the byte string provided to the initial constructor to test that the values changed?

You can only call ```.replace_with``` as a pquery object. In order to get the copy and the ```href``` of the ```hyperlink```, I have to unwrap the pquery object, thereby losing access to the ```.replace_with``` method. When I refactored the code, I "wrapped" the hyperlink back up into a pquery object, so I could then call ```.replace_with``` directly on the element. Still hacky, but less hacky than the old method.
The default behavior of `joblib.Parallel` is multi-processing over multiple cores, not multi-threading. Could make that clearer here?

OK. I dug in a little more, trying to narrow the scope of the polling loop checks as much as possible. From what I've been able to discover, even the loosest of the python JSON parsers likely to be used derives their exceptions from ValueError, so restricting the scope of the parsing catches to that class seems like an acceptable, desirable middleground. I'm testing that now, beating the heck out of my Vera every way I can think of. So far, so good.

Before this patch, boots/time are read from LCD when sending the response message regardless of the securityLevel. I think this is based on rfc2574 3.1.6.b, and it's useful when a non-authoritative SNMP engine send a discovery request message to learn the authoritative SNMP engine's information.

Also I insert some debug log of the response to noAuthNoPriv discovery request message.

I find this similar message confusing:

     _failure_message = ' failed; it passed {0} out of the required {1} times.'

As it will say:

     xxx failed; it passed 0 out of the required 1 times

It reads weird because the first work ("passed") is affirmative. But the following 0 makes it a negative.  I think this would be easier to understand

     failed; test must pass X out of Y runs, but only passed Z times.

It's also a bit misleading in that if say a test needs to pass 4 out of 5 times... if it fails 2 out of the first three, the final 2 attempts will be skipped and the output will read:

     failed; it passed 1 out of the required 4 times

leaving it unsaid that it stopped at 3 attempts. So I'd amend my proposed text to include an additional message only in cases where the full number of attempts weren't used

     failed; test must pass X out of Y times, but only passed Z times with just P attempts remaining

     failed; test must pass 4 out of 5 times, but only passed 1 times [sic] with 2 attempts remaining

The default here seems a bit fragile. The compression ratio could easily be more that 10x, the easiest example is a huge file with only zeros in it.

If you want to be more robust, the solutions I can think of, in order of increasing complexity:
  1) Do the decompression in a loop, with exponentially increasing buffer sizes, until a limit is reached. The limit can be a command-line argument with a reasonable default, e.g. 1GB
  2) Add a python binding for the BrotliDecompressedSize()  and try to use that. Drawback is that it does not work if there are many metablocks, so you would have to fall back to solution 1)
  3) Add a python binding for the decompression function that uses two callbacks, and then you can allocate more output if it is needed.

Their advice is generally sound, but I don't necessarily agree with all of it.

There are at least three (sometimes conflicting!) priorities to optimize for when designing a component:

* Composability
  * Related to how decoupled a component is from external state - how "pure" it is. This affects how easily other parts of the app can be changed without having side effects on this component.
  * Also related to a having a well-defined interface.
  * A pure component with a well-defined interface can be easily re-used in other parts of the app with different data.
* Well-defined dependencies
  * Especially with dynamically typed languages, being explicit about dependencies is critical to allow future refactoring. If you can't easily search for where some state is used, there's no way to know if changing that logic will cause regressions.
  * A pure component with no external dependencies is usually - but not always - the best strategy.
  * If a pure component is not possible, external dependencies should be enumerated as explicitly as possible
* Minimizing boilerplate
  * Code is redundant or repetitive can be difficult to refactor
  * Boilerplate leads to copy-paste coding which can introduce bugs
  * When a large percentage of code is boilerplate and doesn't relate to the core logic of a component, it hinders readability

Different situations lend to different relative priorities. For example, the top-level "page" component in an app, a widely shared core widget, and a single-use helper component each has different forces affecting the design. 

-----

So with regard to their specific advice:

> Close to the top of the nested view hierarchy, a special kind of view listens for events that are broadcast by the stores that it depends on. We call this a controller-view, as it provides the glue code to get the data from the stores and to pass this data down the chain of its descendants. We might have one of these controller-views governing any significant section of the page.

This is an excellent strategy that have been actively trying to follow in Kolibri: try to keep Vuex getters high in the component hierarchy, and pass props down to keep sub-components composable and decoupled.

In this particular case, the individual pages (`defaultLanguageForm`, `facilityNameForm`, etc) are more appropriate as the "controller-view" glue components:

* each of them requires a specific subset of all possible page states. By moving the vuex getters to the individual pages, the dependencies become much better-defined
* passing the entire page into each of the top level pages doesn't do anything useful in terms of composability or well-defined interfaces, and it adds boilerplate.

> We often pass the entire state of the store down the chain of views in a single object, allowing different descendants to use what they need. In addition to keeping the controller-like behavior at the top of the hierarchy, and thus keeping our descendant views as functionally pure as possible, passing down the entire state of the store in a single object also has the effect of reducing the number of props we need to manage.

I don't agree with this strategy. They seem to be optimizing primarily for reduced boilerplate at the cost of well-defined interfaces and explicit dependencies. Components designed like this might be technically "pure", but passing the complete page state object down the component hierarchy does nothing to help with composability or well-defined dependencies.

Note that any refactoring at the app level might break the component - it's tightly coupled to the global application state schema. Furthermore the particular sub-state that the component is dependent on is defined implicitly rather than enumerated explicitly, so it's hard to even know if the component would break due to some particular app-level refactor.

```python
logger.info(The %sth generation, population=%s", self._cur_generation, population_size)
```
